html:
The poisoned sheep — Muhammad's multi-year illness Prophetic Character Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Abu Dawud #4508
"A Jewish woman brought a poisoned sheep (meat) to the Messenger of Allah, and he ate some of it... He asked her about that, and she said: 'I wanted to kill you.' He said: 'Allah would never give you the power to do that'... And I always found it (the effect of that poison) in the uvula of the Messenger of Allah."

What the hadith says

A Jewish woman from Khaybar served Muhammad a poisoned sheep. He ate, questioned her, and she confessed the attempt. Multiple hadiths record that the poison's effect persisted for years and contributed to his final illness.

Why this is a problem

  1. "Allah would never give you the power" is immediately falsified. Muhammad attributes his multi-year illness and ultimately his death to the poison — the declaration of divine protection did not hold.
  2. Parallel narrations contradict on her fate. One account says he did not punish her; Bukhari parallels record her executed after a companion died from the same meal. The tradition cannot agree whether she lived or died.

Why it fails

A warning that arrived too late to prevent ingestion is a miracle that did not fully work. The companion who ate the meat died; Muhammad survived with a years-long injury — divine protection is partial at best. The "Allah would never give them power" promise is in direct tension with a multi-year poison injury caused by exactly the human actors the protection was supposed to prevent.

Chapter: "Abusing And Beating A Captive (And Confession)" Women Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud Book 14, Chapter 117
[Chapter heading:] "Regarding Abusing And Beating A Captive, (And Confession)"

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud dedicates a named chapter to beating and abusing captives to extract confessions. The chapter's existence signals this was a sufficiently standard practice to require legal regulation rather than prohibition.

Why this is a problem

  1. Regulation of abuse is not prohibition of it. A chapter titled "Beating A Captive (And Confession)" legitimises the practice by categorising it as a legal topic with proper procedures.
  2. Modern Muslim spokespeople frequently assert Islam forbids torture. Abu Dawud's chapter heading stands in direct contradiction — the parenthetical "(And Confession)" signals coercion of a desired outcome, not punishment of a proven offense.

Why it fails

The chapter's title is its candid disclosure: Abu Dawud did not title it "On the Prohibition of Abusing Captives" — he titled it on the procedure for doing so. The silent evidence of a hadith collection's chapter headings is its most honest statement about the tradition's moral assumptions.

"To Kill A Captive With An Arrow" — Abu Dawud's chapter title Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Abu Dawud Book 14, Chapter 120
[Chapter heading:] "To Kill A Captive With An Arrow"

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud's Book of Jihad includes a chapter affirming the permissibility of executing a bound captive by arrow rather than sword.

Why this is a problem

  1. Arrow-execution of a bound captive is target practice. The captive cannot defend himself or flee; the dignity of even an unjust execution is removed.
  2. Combined with Chapter 117 on beating captives for confession, Abu Dawud's jurisprudence of captive treatment is comprehensive — torture for information, then arrow-death. The category's existence presupposes tied captives as a regular population requiring execution-method guidance.

Why it fails

A universal ethics does not need a chapter on how to execute tied-up captives efficiently. That Abu Dawud addressed the method, rather than questioning the category, shows the tradition had already answered the foundational moral question in the wrong direction.

"Do not go to extremes in cutting" — female circumcision hadith Women Prophetic Character Strange / Obscure Strong Abu Dawud #5271
"A woman used to circumcise females in Al-Madinah, and the Prophet said to her: 'Do not go to extremes in cutting, for that is better for the woman and more liked by the husband.'" (Abu Dawud grades it Da'if but preserves it; many Shafi'i jurists consider it binding.)

What the hadith says

Female circumcision was practiced in Muhammad's Medina. Rather than prohibiting it, the Prophet gave procedural guidance: don't cut too deeply, because leaving some tissue is better for the woman and more liked by the husband.

Why this is a problem

  1. The hadith permits FGM by regulating it rather than prohibiting it. The stated rationale includes "more liked by the husband" — a woman's body is being cut and a key reason for moderation is spousal preference.
  2. The chain's weakness did not prevent application. UNICEF estimates approximately 200 million women alive today have undergone FGM; a significant proportion are Muslim, and this hadith has provided textual cover across Shafi'i and Shafi'i-influenced traditions for centuries.

Why it fails

The moral test is whether, confronted with the cutting of girls' genitals, a prophet forbade it or regulated it. The text records regulation. "Do not go to extremes" is not the same as "do not do it." Shafi'i jurisprudence historically read this as obligatory or recommended, which is closer to the plain text — and over 200 million women are the evidence that it was not received as a soft discouragement.

The Quran was revealed in seven variant readings Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #1475
"This Qur'an has been revealed in seven Ahruf, so recite whatever is convenient of it."

What the hadith says

The Quran was revealed in seven different variant readings. Reciters could use any variant; divine-name endings could be swapped between readings.

Why this is a problem

  1. "One perfectly preserved Quran" cannot coexist with "seven equally valid revealed variants." Uthman burned six of seven God-given readings to produce the standardised text — a human caliph edited divinely-revealed material.
  2. Ibn Mas'ud and Ubayy ibn Ka'b had different Qurans, both certified by Muhammad. Ibn Mas'ud's codex lacked two complete surahs (113 and 114) present in Uthman's standard. That is not phonetic variation; it is substantive difference.

Why it fails

The "dialectal variants only" reading is a post-Uthmanic apologetic that classical scholars themselves disputed — the range of what counts as an "ahruf" variant has never been settled. The preservation guarantee of Q 15:9 either means the six destroyed variants were not preserved (the guarantee failed) or they were not truly revealed (the hadith's "seven ahruf" is wrong). Both horns of the dilemma damage the tradition.

Blind man killed his slave-concubine for cursing Muhammad — no retaliation Prophetic Character Violence Women Strong Abu Dawud #4361
"He took a dagger, placed it on her belly, pressed it, and killed her... The Prophet said: 'Oh be witness, no retaliation is payable for her blood.'"

What the hadith says

A blind Muslim killed his slave-concubine — mother of his children — for insulting Muhammad. Muhammad declared no blood-money was owed and no retaliation was due, establishing the canonical foundation for blasphemy-killing law.

Why this is a problem

  1. It is the canonical foundation of blasphemy-death jurisprudence. The principle that insulting the Prophet removes the offender's legal protection, and that the killer faces no consequence, traces directly to this hadith.
  2. The victim was doubly vulnerable. Enslaved and female, she had no legal standing to defend herself. Muhammad's absolution of her killer has produced fourteen centuries of blasphemy-killing jurisprudence across multiple jurisdictions.

Why it fails

The "exceptional severity" framing is exactly the problem. A ruling that removes legal protection from an enslaved mother because she verbally insulted someone places the Prophet's honour above a human life. The canonical record is that Muhammad heard about a man killing his sleeping slave-concubine and said: no consequence. Pakistan's blasphemy vigilantism follows directly from this precedent.

Kinana tortured with chest-fire, beheaded; Muhammad married his widow that day Prophetic Character Violence Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Ibn Hisham, Sira; Abu Dawud Khaybar corpus
"'Torture him until you extract what he has.' Zubair kindled a fire on his chest until his breath was almost gone. Then he was beheaded."

What the hadith says

At Khaybar, Kinana ibn al-Rabi was tortured with fire on his chest to extract hidden treasure, then beheaded. Muhammad ordered the torture, then married Kinana's widow Safiyyah that same day — offering her own freedom as her dowry.

Why this is a problem

  1. The torture was ordered for financial extraction, not security. Muhammad's explicit instruction — "torture him until you extract what he has" — names the motive. The victim was then killed anyway, making the torture an addition to the execution, not a substitute for it.
  2. Marrying the widow the same day completes the sequence. Muhammad ordered the torture, executed the husband, and consummated his marriage to Safiyyah on the night of her husband's death. A modern ethics that takes consent seriously cannot treat the widow of a man you tortured this morning as freely choosing marriage to you this evening.

Why it fails

Treaty breach justifies execution; it does not justify fire-on-chest torture as an extraction technique. The canonical record specifies the motive and method in detail — these are not incidental. The Safiyyah marriage cannot be separated from this context: the same man ordered both the torture-execution and the marriage proposal, on the same day.

Abu Rafi killed in his bed; his wife silenced with a drawn sword Prophetic Character Violence Strong Bukhari parallel; Abu Dawud related
"They entered his room at night and killed him in his bed. When his wife cried out, we showed her the sword."

What the hadith says

A Muslim assassination team entered Abu Rafi's home at night, killed him while he slept, and silenced his wife's screams by brandishing a drawn sword. The operation was conducted on Muhammad's authorisation.

Why this is a problem

  1. Night-bed assassination is archetypal treachery by any ancient warfare standard. Pre-Islamic Arab codes distinguished honorable combat from killing an unarmed sleeping man. Threatening the wife to prevent her outcry adds hostage-taking to the list.
  2. It is preserved as a model operation, not an aberration. The canonical record presents this as a successful mission authorised by the Prophet — a template cited across Islamic history for targeted killing of opponents.

Why it fails

The "combatant" classification does not address the method — a night-raid into a sleeping man's bedroom with the wife intimidated to prevent outcry. The operation is preserved as Prophetic sunnah, meaning not merely narrated history but a model of ethically permissible action. A religion whose founding biography includes night-raid bedroom assassinations as exemplary conduct has embedded the method in its template.

"Their houses are better for them" — five hadiths eroding women's mosque access Women Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Abu Dawud #565
"Do not prevent the female servants of Allah from visiting the mosques of Allah." (#566)

"Do not prevent your women from visiting the mosque; but their houses are better for them." (#567)

"If the Messenger of Allah had seen what the women have invented, he would have prevented them from visiting the mosque, as the women of the children of Israel were prevented." — Aisha (#569)

"It is more excellent for a woman to pray in her house than in her courtyard, and more excellent for her to pray in her private chamber than in her house." — attributed to Muhammad (#570)

What the hadith says

Abu Dawud groups six hadiths on women and mosque attendance. They move from a direct command not to prevent women (#566), to a preference that reverses the practical effect (#567), to Aisha's conditional retroactive ban (#569), to a prayer-quality hierarchy placing the innermost chamber above the mosque (#570).

Why this is a problem

  1. "Do not prevent them" and "their houses are better" are operationally incompatible as guidance. Classical jurisprudence used the preference clause to pressure women into staying home while technically preserving the prohibition-on-prohibition.
  2. Aisha's conditional ban is the most devastating piece. The most authoritative female transmitter in the hadith corpus states Muhammad would have banned women from mosques if he could see how they had changed — providing backward-licensing authority for restriction by claiming to know his counterfactual intent.

Why it fails

Demoting Aisha's #569 to mere personal opinion while retaining her authority everywhere else in the corpus is selective application the hadith sciences do not support. The real-world distribution — near-universal de facto exclusion of women from main prayer halls across the Muslim world until very recently — is the historical resolution this canonical cluster produced. A nominal permission structured by qualifying mechanisms is operationally indistinguishable from prohibition.

Sprinkle water for a baby boy's urine, wash for a baby girl's — al-Shafi'i explains: Eve was made from Adam's rib Women Ritual Absurdities Internal Contradictions Logical Inconsistency Pre-Islamic Origins Gross / Vile Strong Ibn Majah #256
"Water should be sprinkled over the urine of a baby boy, and the urine of a baby girl should be washed." (#256–261, six independent chains)

[Al-Shafi'i's etiology, embedded at #259:] "I asked al-Shafi'i: when the two types of water are the same, why the difference? He said: 'The urine of the boy is of water and clay, but the urine of the girl is of flesh and blood.' Then he said: 'When Allah created Adam, He created Eve from his short rib — so the boy's urine is from water and clay, and the girl's urine is from flesh and blood.'"

What the hadith says

Six independent chains establish that a nursing infant boy's urine requires only light sprinkling; a nursing infant girl's requires full washing. Al-Shafi'i, when asked why, grounds the asymmetry in a creation-myth etiology: boys are made of Adam's clay, girls of Eve's flesh-and-blood derivation from his rib.

Why this is a problem

  1. The biological claim is empirically false. Infant urine is biochemically near-identical across sexes at the nursing stage. The rule encodes gender discrimination at the diaper stage with no biological basis, justified by a founding imam's creation-myth derivation.
  2. The Shafi'i and Hanbali schools apply the differential ritual burden as operative law. Six chains in Ibn Majah alone, with parallels in Abu Dawud and Nasa'i, structurally embed the ruling. Al-Shafi'i's explicit question-and-answer format forces a literal rather than metaphorical reading of the etiology.

Why it fails

Al-Shafi'i's response to the direct objection "but the two waters are the same" was not a pivot to metaphor — it was a literal substance claim followed by a creation-myth derivation. A legal system that imposes greater cleaning burdens on infant girls based on the Adam-rib narrative has disclosed the ontological hierarchy on which it operates.

Women who wail at funerals condemned to a garment of pitch and flaming fire Women Morality Ritual Pre-Islamic Origins Internal Contradictions Strong Ibn Majah #1315
"Wailing is one of the affairs of the Days of Ignorance — if the woman who wails dies without having repented, Allah will cut for her a garment of pitch and a shirt of flaming fire." (#1315)

"The deceased is punished for the wailing over him." (#1327)

[At a funeral, Muhammad sees a wailing woman; Umar shouts at her:] "Leave her alone, O Umar, for the eye weeps and the heart is afflicted, and the bereavement is recent." (#1321)

What the hadith says

Ibn Majah preserves a cluster condemning female ritual mourning as pre-Islamic ignorance and threatening practitioners with Hellfire — while simultaneously preserving a hadith where Muhammad rebukes Umar for silencing a wailing woman at a funeral and permits her to grieve.

Why this is a problem

  1. The internal contradiction is preserved without resolution. In the same collection, #1321 shows Muhammad permitting the exact behaviour ##1315-1320 condemn to eternal fire. The canonical text preserves both without reconciliation.
  2. The "deceased punished by mourning" doctrine violates Q 35:18. "No bearer of burdens shall bear another's burden" is the Quran's explicit statement. That a dead person suffers in the grave for a living relative's weeping directly contradicts Quranic individual-accountability theology.

Why it fails

The texts condemn raising one's voice at calamity and scratching one's face — embodied expressions of extreme sorrow, not a formal-professional category alone. Ibn Majah #1321's contradiction is the canonical record's own evidence that the prohibition overreached: even Muhammad permitted what the surrounding hadiths condemn to eternal fire.

"Satan eats with his left hand" — the prohibition pathologising left-handedness Strange / Obscure Moral Problems Logic Strong Ibn Majah #3002
"Let one of you eat with his right hand and drink with his right hand, and take with his right hand and give with his right hand, for Satan eats with his left hand, drinks with his left hand, gives with his left hand and takes with his left hand." (#3002)

What the hadith says

Two independent chains prohibit eating, drinking, giving, and taking with the left hand — with the explicit stated reason that Satan uses his left hand for all these acts.

Why this is a problem

  1. The doctrine pathologises a natural anatomical variation in roughly 10% of humanity. Left-handedness has genetic and neurological correlates entirely outside individual choice. Left-handed people are commanded to act against their neurology on the stated grounds that their natural handedness mirrors the Devil's.
  2. The prohibition's rationale requires Satan to have a physical left hand. If taken literally, Islamic theology has committed to Satan having eating habits and hand-preferences. If taken metaphorically, the prohibition's own stated reason evaporates.

Why it fails

If the prohibition were about hygiene or bodily discipline independent of Satan, the hadiths would say so — but both chains explicitly name Satan as the reason. Naturally left-handed people are condemned to choose between their neurology (mirroring Satan) or physical strain in every meal and transaction. Compulsory right-handedness enforced by physical correction of left-handed children is the operational history these two chains produced.
